1	---
2	title: Why DDx
3	description: "The new reality of agent-driven development, the pain it creates, the physics behind it, and the four primitives DDx provides in response."
4	weight: 1
5	---
6	
7	# Why DDx
8	
9	Agents now write most of the code. The tools, conventions, and habits we built for human-only teams quietly stop working when the team is half humans and half AI. DDx exists because the substrate underneath agent-driven development is missing — and without it, shipping software with agents degrades into prompt-and-pray.
10	
11	This page lays out the argument: what changed, what hurts, why it hurts, and what we built in response.
12	
13	## 1. The New Reality
14	
15	A few things are true now that weren't true two years ago:
16	
17	- **Agents execute, humans direct.** A working developer steers, reviews, and approves; the agent writes, edits, and runs the commands. The ratio of agent-authored to human-authored lines keeps climbing.
18	- **Every invocation starts from zero.** Agents have no persistent memory between runs. Whatever context an agent needs to do good work has to be assembled and handed in every time.
19	- **Output volume outpaces review capacity.** A single developer can dispatch more work in an hour than they could write in a week. The bottleneck moves from typing to deciding what to keep.
20	- **Cost is now a first-order constraint.** Token spend is real money on every iteration. Choosing the right model for the job is a daily concern, not a one-time configuration.
21	- **Work is parallel and interleaved.** Multiple agents drain a queue concurrently; humans pick up some beads, agents pick up others. Coordination has to survive concurrency.
22	
23	The shape of "writing software" has changed. The tools haven't.
24	
25	## 2. Six Pain Points
26	
27	What teams hit, in the order they hit them:
28	
29	### Constant context re-explanation
30	Every agent invocation requires re-stating the same project context: what we're building, the conventions, the prior decisions, the specs that govern this change. Without a shared, durable artifact substrate, this overhead repeats forever. Each session starts from zero.
31	
32	### Specs and code drift apart
33	A spec is updated; the code isn't. Or the code changes; the spec doesn't. Without explicit relationships between artifacts and a way to detect staleness, drift accumulates silently until a major bug or onboarding session exposes it.
34	
35	### Work-tracking reinvented per tool
36	Every workflow tool, every methodology, every project ends up rebuilding the same issue store, dependency tracker, and ready-queue from scratch. The work substrate is local infrastructure that should be shared, not re-implemented per tool.
37	
38	### Execution evidence evaporates
39	Agents run, produce output, and that output disappears into a terminal scrollback. There's no durable record of what was run, what model was used, what tokens were spent, what files changed, or what the result was. Without evidence, evaluation, debugging, and learning all degrade to anecdote.
40	
41	### Routine work runs on premium models
42	Without capability-aware routing, every bead runs on the same model — usually the strongest one configured. Cheap, reliable, mechanical work pays the price of frontier-model inference. There's no signal on which model is the cheapest one that reliably closes which kind of bead.
43	
44	### Agents re-learn each project's CLI
45	Every project has a slightly different shape. Without a discoverable, consistent surface, agents waste tokens probing for commands, conventions, and entry points. The harness keeps re-deriving facts the project already knows.
46	
47	## 3. The Root Cause
48	
49	Step back from the symptoms and the underlying issue is one sentence:
50	
51	> **Agents execute in isolation with no shared memory.**
52	
53	Every pain above is downstream of this. Context re-explanation is the absence of shared memory across sessions. Spec/code drift is the absence of shared memory across artifacts. Work-tracking reinvention is the absence of shared memory across tools. Evidence loss is the absence of shared memory across runs. Cost overspend is the absence of shared memory about what worked at what tier. Project re-learning is the absence of shared memory about the project's own surface.
54	
55	Patching symptom-by-symptom doesn't fix it. What's needed is a substrate that gives agent work a place to stand — durable, file-based, agent-discoverable, and shared across every invocation.
56	
57	DDx answers with four primitives:
58	
59	1. **Artifacts** — versioned, related, discoverable project knowledge. Documents, diagrams, prompts, personas, and other media live in the repo with explicit identity and relationships. The artifact graph tracks staleness when an upstream artifact changes.
60	2. **Beads** — the unit of work. Self-contained items with acceptance criteria and a dependency DAG. The ready/blocked queue is the interface between intent and execution. Beads make intent legible to humans and agents alike.
61	3. **Tracked execution (`ddx work`)** — drain the bead queue. Each attempt runs in an isolated worktree, captures evidence (model, tokens, files changed, exit metadata), and either merges back on success or preserves the result for inspection. The history persists.
62	4. **Skills** — a single, consolidated agent-facing surface. One DDx skill teaches an agent how to operate the toolkit: bead triage, agent dispatch, plugin installation, evidence inspection. Agents stop probing; they read the skill once.
63	
64	These four primitives are the shared memory the agent-driven workflow was missing.
65	
66	## 4. The Physics
67	
68	Underneath the pain points and the response are six load-bearing claims about how software gets built and how generative AI behaves. We call these the *physics* — facts you can either respect or pay the cost of ignoring.
69	
70	### Physics of Software
71	
72	1. **Abstraction is the lever.** Multi-level artifact stacks with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations. If the artifact stack isn't there, every invocation rebuilds context from scratch and gets it slightly wrong.
73	
74	2. **Software is iteration over tracked work.** Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. This pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional. Untracked work cannot be drained, evaluated, or coordinated across multiple agents.
75	
76	3. **Methodology is plural.** Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. The platform must provide primitives that any methodology composes; opinions belong in optional plugins.
77	
78	### Physics of Generative AI
79	
80	4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Quality degrades as the context window fills. Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and "cheapest model that reliably closes the bead" are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations to add later. Treating LLM calls as if they were deterministic function calls produces brittle workflows and surprise bills.
81	
82	5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what was captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
83	
84	### The Intersection
85	
86	6. **Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum.** Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. Handoffs run in both directions, at every level of the stack — and the toolkit at the seam decides whether those handoffs are smooth or expensive.
87	
88	The four primitives are the choices DDx makes in response to these six. Artifacts honor #1. Beads honor #2 and #3. Tracked execution honors #4 and #5. Skills honor #6.
89	
90	## 5. DDx as the Response
91	
92	Put it together and the operating model is straightforward:
93	
94	- **Project knowledge lives in the repo as artifacts.** Plain files, plain git, agent-discoverable. Identity and relationships are explicit, not inferred. Staleness is detected, not discovered the hard way.
95	- **Work flows through beads.** Anything an agent does is against a bead. The bead carries acceptance criteria; review checks the criteria, not memory of the conversation. The queue is the contract between intent and execution.
96	- **Agents run via `ddx work`.** The queue drains in isolated worktrees, evidence is captured automatically, and successful results merge back. Failed or timed-out runs are preserved for inspection. Token cost, model choice, and outcome are all recorded.
97	- **Agents learn the toolkit through one skill.** One consolidated DDx skill replaces a fleet of project-specific instructions. Every project that uses DDx looks the same to the agent.
98	- **Everything is local-first.** `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch the project root. There's no home-directory state, no machine-wide opinions, no proprietary backend. Cloning the repo gives you the whole picture.
99	- **Every primitive is workflow-agnostic.** DDx ships no methodology. Phases, gates, and supervisory loops belong in plugins, not in the platform.
100	
101	The result: agents stop starting from zero. Work has a place to live. Evidence persists. The substrate that agent-driven development was missing is on disk, in the repo, in plain files — exactly where everything else already is.
102	
103	That's why DDx exists.
104	
105	---
106	
107	Ready to try it? Start with [Get Started](/docs/getting-started/) or browse the [Features](/features/).
108

2	ddx:
3	  id: product-vision
4	---
5	# Product Vision: DDx
6	
7	**Version:** 2.1.0
8	**Date:** 2026-04-29
9	**Status:** Active
10	
11	## Core Thesis
12	
13	> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
14	>
15	> Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.
16	
17	## The Productivity Shift
18	
19	Agentic AI compresses what used to be person-weeks of engineering into hours. The bottleneck moves: writing code is no longer the binding constraint — keeping intent, artifacts, and evidence coherent across many parallel agent invocations is. Teams that hit this new ceiling keep hitting the same six pain points, and every team independently rebuilds the same infrastructure to climb past them. DDx ships that infrastructure once.
20	
21	Each pain point below maps to a specific DDx capability — the list is bounded by what the platform actually addresses, not an open-ended catalog.
22	
23	1. **Context assembly is manual.** Composing the right persona + pattern + spec + prior evidence into agent context is ad hoc and slow. *(DDx: artifact library + composition.)*
24	2. **Documents drift silently.** When an upstream document changes, dependent artifacts go stale with no detection or reconciliation. *(DDx: artifact graph + staleness + reconciliation beads.)*
25	3. **Work tracking is reinvented.** Every workflow tool reimplements issue storage, dependency DAGs, and ready/blocked queues. *(DDx: bead tracker + JSONL interchange.)*
26	4. **Agent runs leave no shared trace.** Each tool grows its own dispatch, logging, and evidence shape; nothing carries between invocations. *(DDx: three-layer run architecture on a unified on-disk substrate.)*
27	5. **Cost of agentic work is invisible.** Token spend is a first-order constraint with no shared signal on the cheapest model that reliably closes beads. *(DDx: cost-tier routing + token awareness.)*
28	6. **Skills and plugins don't compose.** Each project reinvents its agent instructions, skills, and supporting mechanics from scratch. *(DDx: single `ddx` skill + project-local plugin install.)*
29	
30	### Physics of Software
31	
32	1. **Abstraction is the lever.** Multi-level artifact stacks (vision → spec → test → code) with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations.
33	2. **Software is iteration over tracked work.** Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. Pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional.
34	3. **Methodology is plural.** Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, HELIX, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. DDx provides primitives (artifacts, runs, beads) that any methodology composes.
35	
36	### Physics of Generative AI
37	
38	4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
39	5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
40	
41	### The Intersection — DDx's Reason to Exist
42	
43	6. **Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum.** Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. DDx is the toolkit at the seam — handoffs in both directions, at every level.
44	
45	## The Three-Layer Stack
46	
47	DDx is one layer in a three-project stack with explicit boundaries:
48	
49	| Layer | Project | Owns |
50	|-------|---------|------|
51	| Platform | **DDx** (this repo) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync |
52	| Workflow | **HELIX** | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, bounded actions, methodology |
53	| Quality | **Dun** | Check discovery, execution, agent-friendly output |
54	
55	DDx provides primitives. HELIX and others provide opinions. Dun verifies the
56	result. Each layer is independently useful and replaceable.
57	
58	## Mission
59	
60	Make artifacts the unit of software development. Provide the shared
61	infrastructure that developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and
62	deliver the artifacts agents produce and consume to build software.
63	
64	## What DDx Provides
65	
66	- **Artifact library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts in
67	  the repository. Documents, diagrams, wireframes, prompts, images, and other
68	  media; generators checked into git with source-hash-driven regeneration.
69	  Personas, patterns, templates, MCP server registry. Artifacts may be produced
70	  or consumed by humans and agents alike; DDx manages identity and provenance
71	  across all four combinations.
72	- **Multi-media artifact graph** — artifact identity, relationships, and content
73	  hashing across document and non-document media. Any file with a sidecar
74	  `.ddx.yaml` is a first-class artifact.
75	- **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues,
76	  and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute.
77	- **Three-layer run architecture** — `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx
78	  try` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). Agent
79	  invocation is upstream; DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence bundling, and
80	  drain.
81	- **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch
82	  `<projectRoot>`. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`. No home-directory
83	  state, no machine-wide opinions.
84	- **Single `ddx` skill** — DDx ships one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One
85	  surface for agents to learn.
86	
87	## What DDx Is Not
88	
89	- **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types.
90	  Workflow tools (HELIX) own those.
91	- **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend.
92	- **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever the user works.
93	- **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out
94	  contract plugs in.
95	
96	## Key Differentiators
97	
98	### vs. Ad-Hoc Agentic Coding
99	
100	Code-as-system-of-record produces point changes without coupling awareness, no
101	structured intent, and constant context re-explanation. DDx makes documents the
102	system of record for intent and architecture; code remains the system of record
103	for implementation.
104	
105	### vs. Code-Only Agent Tools
106	
107	Without an abstraction hierarchy, functionality accumulates in non-orthogonal
108	interfaces and agents cannot reason about cross-cutting impact. DDx gives every
109	change a defined place and a known impact boundary.
110	
111	### vs. Traditional Documentation-Driven Development
112	
113	Prior DDD failed because documents went stale. DDx counters that with
114	relationships, content hashing, beads as reconciliation tasks, and adversarial
115	review.
116	
117	## Design Philosophy
118	
119	### Platform vs Workflow Boundary
120	
121	The hardest discipline is keeping platform code free of methodology opinions.
122	The boundary is concrete:
123	
124	| DDx (Platform) | Workflow Tool (e.g., HELIX) |
125	|----------------|------------------------------|
126	| Document storage, versioning, sync | Artifact types and templates |
127	| Bead CRUD, dependency DAG, ready queue | Phase definitions, gates, ratchets |
128	| Agent dispatch, quorum, session logging | Methodology prompts, supervisory loops |
129	| Persona binding mechanism | Which personas fulfill which roles |
130	| Execute-loop runtime | When to run it, what to do with results |
131	
132	If a feature request would push opinions into the CLI, it belongs in a plugin.
133	
134	### Multi-Directional Iteration
135	
136	The artifact hierarchy is a set of lenses, not a pipeline. Vision changes
137	propagate down; implementation discoveries feed up; spec refinements ripple
138	both ways. DDx's primitives (beads, document relationships, content hashing)
139	support all three directions equally.
140	
141	### Cost-Tiered Execution
142	
143	Closed-bead throughput per dollar is the optimization target. Cheap models do
144	the work; stronger models review; deterministic checks (Dun) sit at the top of
145	the ladder catching what review missed. The agent service routes by capability,
146	not by name — endpoints with live model discovery, not hardcoded providers.
147	
148	### Three-Layer Run Architecture
149	
150	DDx owns three explicit layered primitives:
151	
152	| Layer | CLI | Owns |
153	|-------|-----|------|
154	| 1 | `ddx run` | Single agent invocation; structured output, tokens, model, exit metadata |
155	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | Bead attempt in isolated worktree; merge or preserve result |
156	| 3 | `ddx work` | Mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection and stop conditions |
157	
158	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
159	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records. Agent invocation is upstream
160	(DDx-agent module via CONTRACT-003); DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence
161	bundling, and drain loop.
162	
163	### Human-Agent Control Slider
164	
165	DDx supports a continuum from full autonomy (drain the queue overnight) to
166	human-driven (agent assists, human commits). The same primitives serve both;
167	the workflow layer chooses where to sit.
168	
169	## Strategic Fit
170	
171	**Why us:** DDx grew out of daily agent-driven development. The problems we
172	solve are the problems we hit shipping software with agents.
173	
174	**Why now:** Document quality is the bottleneck. Every team is independently
175	rebuilding the same document infrastructure. No shared layer exists.
176	
177	**North Star:** Every developer working with AI agents uses DDx, or tools built
178	on DDx, to manage the documents that drive those agents.
179	
180	## Principles
181	
182	See `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md`. The load-bearing ones:
183	
184	Operating principles are the choices DDx makes in response to the physics above.
185	
186	1. **Platform, not methodology** — DDx provides primitives; workflow tools provide opinions.
187	2. **Project-local by default** — install touches the project, not the machine.
188	3. **Artifacts are the product (documents primary, other media supported)** — code is output; artifacts are what you maintain.
189	4. **Bead-driven work** — beads are the unit of work; the queue is the interface.
190	5. **Cost-tiered throughput** — optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability.
191	6. **Git-native, file-first** — plain files, standard git, no lock-in.
192	7. **Agent-agnostic** — any capable harness plugs in via the prompt envelope.
193

input.command: find website/content/features -type f | head -20 && echo "---" && find docs/helix -name "*execute*" -o -name "*loop*" 2>/dev/null | head -20

data.output:
website/content/features/_index.md
---
docs/helix/03-test/executions/EXEC-006-execute-bead-cli-regressions.md
docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-001-execute-bead-supervisor-contract.md

1	---
2	title: Features
3	description: "DDx platform capabilities — stable, beta, and planned."
4	---
5	
6	DDx is a focused set of platform primitives for AI-assisted development. Each capability below carries a maturity badge so you can tell what is solid today from what is still in motion.
7	
8	## Artifact Graph {#artifact-graph} {{< maturity "beta" >}}
9	
10	![Artifact graph view](/ui/feature-artifact-graph.png "Artifact graph — placeholder")
11	
12	Documents are not the only thing a project ships. Diagrams, screenshots, schema files, fixtures, generated assets — all of them carry meaning, and all of them drift. DDx treats every artifact as a first-class node in a single graph: markdown documents directly, and non-markdown files (graphics, diagrams, binaries) through a `.ddx.yaml` sidecar that records kind, provenance, and links.
13	
14	The graph exists to answer two questions:
15	
16	- **Drift visibility** — which artifacts have moved out from under the documents that reference them, and which references now point at stale or missing targets?
17	- **Impact analysis** — if you change this spec, which features, beads, diagrams, and downstream artifacts are implicated?
18	
19	```
20	ddx doc graph         # show the artifact graph
21	ddx doc audit         # surface drift and broken references
22	```
23	
24	This is the spine the rest of the platform hangs from. Beads reference artifacts; executions produce artifacts; reviews assess artifacts. Keeping the graph honest keeps everything else honest.
25	
26	## Beads & DAG {#beads-dag} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
27	
28	![Bead list with dependency tree](/ui/feature-beads-dag.png "Beads and dependency DAG — placeholder")
29	
30	Beads are the atomic unit of work. Each one carries a title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, state history, and a typed dependency edge to other beads. The dependency graph is a DAG, not a list: `ddx bead ready` returns only beads whose prerequisites are closed, so the queue is always a correct-by-construction work front.
31	
32	```
33	ddx bead create "Add login endpoint" --ac "returns 200 on valid credentials"
34	ddx bead dep add <child> --on <parent>
35	ddx bead ready
36	ddx bead tree <id>
37	```
38	
39	## Execute-Loop (`ddx work`) {#execute-loop} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
40	
41	![Execute-loop draining the queue](/ui/feature-execute-loop.png "ddx work draining the queue — placeholder")
42	
43	`ddx work` drains the bead queue. It picks the next ready bead, runs the configured agent harness inside an isolated git worktree, captures evidence, and merges the result back when acceptance criteria pass. Failing or timed-out runs are preserved as refs for inspection rather than discarded.
44	
45	```
46	ddx work                              # drain the queue
47	ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
48	```
49	
50	Because each iteration runs in its own worktree, parallel runs are safe and a failed run never poisons the main branch.
51	
52	## Evidence Capture {#evidence-capture} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
53	
54	![Execution evidence bundle](/ui/feature-evidence-capture.png "Evidence capture bundle — placeholder")
55	
56	Every execution writes a bundle under `.ddx/executions/<timestamp>-<hash>/` with the prompt, the agent transcript, token and cost telemetry, the diff produced, and the merge outcome. Evidence stays in the repository, attached to the commit, so future reviewers (human or model) can reconstruct what happened without rerunning the agent.
57	
58	```
59	ddx agent log
60	ls .ddx/executions/
61	```
62	
63	## Multi-Model Review {#multi-model-review} {{< maturity "beta" >}}
64	
65	![Multi-model review consensus](/ui/feature-multi-model-review.png "Multi-model review — placeholder")
66	
67	A single model is a single point of view. DDx can dispatch the same prompt — a plan, a diff, a spec — to multiple harnesses (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local models) and aggregate their findings. Quorum modes let you require majority or unanimous agreement before a result is treated as approved.
68	
69	```
70	ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini --prompt review.md
71	```
72	
73	The pattern works for plan review before implementation, diff review before merge, and spec sanity-checks before breakdown into beads.
74	
75	## Skills {#skills} {{< maturity "beta" >}}
76	
77	![Skill listing](/ui/feature-skills.png "Skills — placeholder")
78	
79	Skills are reusable, agent-invocable capabilities packaged alongside the project. They install under `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/` and become available to any harness that supports skill discovery. Plugins ship skills the way they ship templates and prompts — versioned, project-local, no global state.
80	
81	```
82	ddx install <plugin-name>     # plugins can carry skills
83	ls .claude/skills/
84	```
85	
86	## Agent-Agnostic Dispatch {#agent-agnostic-dispatch} {{< maturity "stable" >}}
87	
88	![Agent harness selection](/ui/feature-agent-dispatch.png "Agent-agnostic dispatch — placeholder")
89	
90	DDx talks to agents through a uniform harness interface. Swap Claude for Codex for a local model by changing configuration, not commands. The same `ddx agent run` invocation works across providers; cost, tokens, and latency are recorded the same way regardless of which harness answered.
91	
92	```
93	ddx agent list                # show available harnesses
94	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt prompts/implement.md
95	ddx agent run --harness codex --prompt prompts/implement.md
96	ddx agent doctor              # harness health check
97	```
98	
99	This is what makes cost-tiered routing and multi-model review possible: the rest of the platform never has to care which model is on the other end of the wire.
100

1 --- 2 title: Concepts 3 weight: 2 4 --- 5 6 The ideas behind DDx and document-driven development. 7 8 ## What DDx Is 9 10 DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is the **shared infrastructure 11 platform for document-driven development**. It provides the primitives that 12 developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and deliver the 13 documents AI agents consume to build software. 14 15 The core thesis: **document quality, not agent capability, is the bottleneck.** 16 Well-maintained abstractions — requirements, architecture, design, tests — 17 produce better software than working at the code level alone. DDx encodes that 18 insight into infrastructure. 19 20 ```mermaid 21 flowchart LR 22 Dev["Developer
(maintains documents)"] 23 subgraph Docs["Documents (the product)"] 24 direction TB 25 Specs["Specs"] 26 Personas["Personas"] 27 Patterns["Patterns"] 28 Templates["Templates"] 29 Prompts["Prompts"] 30 end 31 Agent["AI Agent
(harness)"] 32 Code["Code,
tests, artifacts"] 33 34 Dev -->|edits| Docs 35 Docs -->|composed into prompt| Agent 36 Agent -->|produces| Code 37 Code -.->|feedback informs| Docs 38 39 classDef product fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937; 40 class Docs product; 41 ``` 42 43 ## The Three-Layer Stack 44 45 DDx is one layer in a three-project stack with explicit boundaries. Each layer 46 is independently useful and replaceable. 47 48 | Layer | Project | Owns | 49 |-------|---------|------| 50 | **Platform** | [DDx](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync | 51 | **Workflow** | [HELIX](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/helix) | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, methodology | 52 | **Quality** | [Dun](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/dun) | Check discovery, execution, agent-friendly output | 53 54 DDx provides primitives. HELIX and others provide opinions. Dun verifies the 55 result. 56 57 ```mermaid 58 flowchart TB 59 subgraph Workflow["Workflow layer — opinions"] 60 HELIX["HELIX
phases · gates · supervisory dispatch"] 61 Other["...alternative
workflows"] 62 end 63 subgraph Platform["Platform layer — primitives"] 64 DDx["DDx
document library · bead tracker ·
agent dispatch · personas · git sync"] 65 end 66 subgraph Quality["Quality layer — verification"] 67 Dun["Dun
check discovery · execution ·
agent-friendly output"] 68 end 69 70 HELIX -->|consumes primitives| DDx 71 Other -->|consumes primitives| DDx 72 DDx -->|invokes checks| Dun 73 Dun -->|results| DDx 74 75 classDef platform fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e293b; 76 classDef workflow fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#1e293b; 77 classDef quality fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#a21caf,color:#1e293b; 78 class DDx platform; 79 class HELIX,Other workflow; 80 class Dun quality; 81 ``` 82 83 {{< callout type="info" >}} 84 **Why three layers?** Mixing methodology into platform code locks teams into 85 one way of working. Keeping the platform opinion-free lets HELIX evolve, lets 86 alternative workflows exist, and lets DDx serve both fully-autonomous and 87 human-driven teams from the same primitives. 88 {{< /callout >}} 89 90 ## Documents Are the Product 91 92 The fundamental shift: **you maintain documents, agents produce code.** 93 94 In traditional development, code is the primary artifact. In document-driven 95 development, the primary artifacts are the documents that tell agents what to 96 build and how to build it: 97 98 - **Prompts** — instructions that direct agent behavior for specific tasks 99 - **Personas** — behavioral definitions that shape how agents approach work 100 - **Patterns** — proven solutions to recurring problems, written for agent 101 consumption 102 - **Templates** — project and file blueprints with variable substitution 103 - **Specs** — requirements and designs that define what to build 104 105 The quality of agent output follows directly from the quality of these 106 documents. Better documents produce better code, every time. 107 108 ## What DDx Provides 109 110 - **Document library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts 111 in the repository. 112 - **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues, 113 and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute. 114 - **Agent service** — unified harness dispatch (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local 115 models) with quorum review, session logging, and a single prompt envelope. 116 - **Execute-loop** — drain the bead queue with isolated worktrees, automatic 117 review, and recovery. Cheap models do, strong models review. 118 - **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install ` only touch 119 ``. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`. 120 - **Single `ddx` skill** — one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One surface 121 for agents to learn. 122 123 ## What DDx Is Not 124 125 - **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types — 126 workflow tools own those. 127 - **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend. 128 - **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever you already work. 129 - **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out 130 contract plugs in. 131 132 ## Read Next 133 134 - [Principles](principles/) — the load-bearing decisions behind DDx. 135 - [Architecture](architecture/) — how beads, the execute-loop, personas, and 136 the project-local install model fit together. 137 - [Glossary](glossary/) — quick definitions for the terms used across the 138 docs. 139
website/content/docs/concepts/glossary.md:52:gates, supervisory dispatch, and bounded actions. HELIX is one valid

1	---
2	title: Glossary
3	weight: 4
4	---
5	
6	Quick definitions for the terms used across the DDx docs.
7	
8	## Acceptance Criteria
9	
10	The checked items on a bead that define when the bead is complete. Reviews
11	check the criteria, not the developer's memory of the conversation. See
12	[Architecture: Bead Lifecycle](../architecture/#bead-lifecycle).
13	
14	## Agent Service
15	
16	The DDx subsystem that dispatches work to AI harnesses behind a unified
17	prompt envelope. Handles routing, session logging, quorum review, and
18	execute-loop runtime. Surfaced as `ddx agent ...`.
19	
20	## Bead
21	
22	A self-contained unit of work with an ID, title, description, acceptance
23	criteria, labels, and a dependency DAG edge set. Beads live in the project's
24	bead store as JSONL. Anything an agent does should be against a bead.
25	
26	## Bead Store
27	
28	The on-disk JSONL store of beads inside the project (`<projectRoot>/.ddx/beads/`).
29	Diffable, mergeable, branch-aware. Importable from and exportable to `bd`/`br`.
30

data.text: Now I'll make the four edits. Starting with the new concept doc.

input.content:
---
title: Bounded Context Execution
weight: 5
---

How DDx fights **context rot** by running every agent attempt inside a bounded
window — a pattern sometimes called the **Ralph loop**.

## Context Rot

LLMs do not degrade gracefully as their context window fills. Quality drops
well before the hard token limit: the model loses track of earlier
instructions, repeats itself, hallucinates files, and second-guesses prior
decisions. Long-running agent sessions accumulate transcript, tool output,
failed attempts, and partial reasoning — every additional token competes for
attention with the original instructions.

We call this **context rot**: the steady decay in agent output quality as a
single session's context grows. It is not a bug in any one model. It is a
property of how transformer attention scales over a single window, and it
shows up across every frontier model we have measured.

The implication: an agent that has been running for an hour is not the same
agent that started the session. Its judgment has degraded. Its outputs need
more review, not less. And no amount of "remember to..." reminders fixes the
underlying decay — the rot lives in the context, not in the prompt.

## Bounded Context Execution

The fix is structural: keep every unit of agent work inside a fresh,
bounded context window.

**Bounded context execution** is the pattern of:

1. Loading a small, complete contract (the bead description, acceptance
   criteria, and the specific files the work touches) into a clean context.
2. Letting the agent execute against that contract until the contract is
   satisfied or the attempt is abandoned.
3. Discarding the working context. Persistent state — what was done, what was
   learned, what failed — lands on disk as evidence, code, or an updated
   bead, not as transcript carried forward.
4. Starting the next attempt from a fresh window, hydrated only from the
   on-disk substrate.

Each attempt sees only what it needs. Context never accumulates across
attempts. The agent at attempt N+1 is as sharp as the agent at attempt 1
because it is, in the relevant sense, the same agent meeting the work for
the first time.

## The Ralph Loop

The Ralph loop is the name for this iteration shape: **fresh context, bounded
attempt, durable evidence, repeat.** Named after the cartoon character who
introduces himself anew in every scene, the loop trades long-session
"intelligence" for short-session reliability.

A single Ralph iteration:

```
read bead → load minimal context → attempt the work
         → capture evidence → close or re-queue → exit
```

The next iteration starts cold. It re-reads the bead. It re-reads the files.
It does not remember what the previous attempt tried, except through whatever
that attempt wrote down — a commit, a note in the bead, a `no_changes_rationale.txt`,
a `<review-findings>` block threaded into the next prompt.

This is slower per iteration than a long session. It is dramatically more
reliable across iterations, and it is the only known way to drain a queue of
non-trivial work without quality collapsing partway through.

## How DDx Implements It

DDx is built around this loop:

- **Beads** are the bounded contract. Each bead carries its own description,
  acceptance criteria, and dependency context — enough to be executed
  in isolation.
- **`ddx agent execute-bead`** runs a single bounded attempt in an isolated
  git worktree. The agent sees the bead, the worktree, and a tight set of
  instructions. When the attempt ends, the worktree's state is captured as
  evidence and either merged or preserved.
- **`ddx work` (the execute-loop)** drains the bead queue by repeatedly
  invoking `execute-bead`. Each iteration is a fresh agent invocation with a
  fresh context. There is no shared session state across beads.
- **Evidence under `.ddx/executions/`** is the durable memory between
  iterations. Tokens, model, files changed, exit reason, review findings —
  all on disk, all readable by the next iteration if relevant.
- **Review-findings threading** is how learning crosses the boundary without
  carrying transcript. When a review rejects an attempt, the findings are
  written into the next prompt as a `<review-findings>` block. The agent
  does not need the prior transcript; it needs the verdict.

The result is a substrate that respects context rot instead of fighting it.
DDx does not try to make any single agent invocation smarter or longer-lived.
It makes the loop around the agent the smart part, and keeps each invocation
small, fresh, and accountable to a contract on disk.

## See Also

- [Why DDx](/why/) — the pain that this loop addresses, in plain terms.
- [Principles](../principles/) — the load-bearing decisions behind DDx.
- [Execute-loop feature](/features/#execute-loop) — the `ddx work` surface.

input.old_string:
4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
input.new_string:
4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
6. **Context rot is real; bounded context execution is the response.** LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills — well before the hard token limit. Long-running agent sessions accumulate transcript, tool noise, and failed attempts that compete with the original instructions; the agent at hour one is not the agent at minute one. The structural fix is **bounded context execution** — also known as the **Ralph loop**: every unit of work runs in a fresh, narrowly-scoped context against an explicit contract (a bead with acceptance criteria), with persistent state landing on disk as evidence rather than carried forward as transcript. DDx implements this with `ddx agent execute-bead` (single bounded attempt in an isolated worktree) and `ddx work` (queue drain that re-enters the loop with a fresh context per bead). See `website/content/docs/concepts/bounded-context-execution.md` for the full treatment.

data.text: Now update the why root cause section:

input.old_string:
> **Agents execute in isolation with no shared memory.**

Every pain above is downstream of this. Context re-explanation is the absence of shared memory across sessions. Spec/code drift is the absence of shared memory across artifacts. Work-tracking reinvention is the absence of shared memory across tools. Evidence loss is the absence of shared memory across runs. Cost overspend is the absence of shared memory about what worked at what tier. Project re-learning is the absence of shared memory about the project's own surface.

Patching symptom-by-symptom doesn't fix it. What's needed is a substrate that gives agent work a place to stand — durable, file-based, agent-discoverable, and shared across every invocation.
input.new_string:
> **Agents execute in isolation with no shared memory.**

Every pain above is downstream of this. Context re-explanation is the absence of shared memory across sessions. Spec/code drift is the absence of shared memory across artifacts. Work-tracking reinvention is the absence of shared memory across tools. Evidence loss is the absence of shared memory across runs. Cost overspend is the absence of shared memory about what worked at what tier. Project re-learning is the absence of shared memory about the project's own surface.

This isolation is not optional, and trying to fight it with longer sessions makes things worse. LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills — a phenomenon we call **context rot**. Transcript, tool output, failed attempts, and partial reasoning accumulate and compete with the original instructions. The agent at hour one is sharper than the agent at hour three, even at the same model and the same prompt. Long-running agent sessions trade reliability for the illusion of continuity.

The structural fix is **bounded context execution** — also known as the **Ralph loop**: every unit of agent work runs in a fresh, narrowly-scoped context against an explicit contract, and durable state lives on disk as evidence rather than as transcript carried forward. The next attempt re-enters cold, reads what it needs from the substrate, and executes against the same kind of contract. Iteration becomes reliable because no single iteration has to remember anything.

Patching symptom-by-symptom doesn't fix it. What's needed is a substrate that gives agent work a place to stand — durable, file-based, agent-discoverable, and shared across every invocation — plus a loop shape that respects context rot instead of fighting it.

data.text: Now update the execute-loop feature page:

input.old_string:
`ddx work` drains the bead queue. It picks the next ready bead, runs the configured agent harness inside an isolated git worktree, captures evidence, and merges the result back when acceptance criteria pass. Failing or timed-out runs are preserved as refs for inspection rather than discarded.

```
ddx work                              # drain the queue
ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
```

Because each iteration runs in its own worktree, parallel runs are safe and a failed run never poisons the main branch.
input.new_string:
`ddx work` drains the bead queue. It picks the next ready bead, runs the configured agent harness inside an isolated git worktree, captures evidence, and merges the result back when acceptance criteria pass. Failing or timed-out runs are preserved as refs for inspection rather than discarded.

```
ddx work                              # drain the queue
ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
```

Because each iteration runs in its own worktree, parallel runs are safe and a failed run never poisons the main branch.

### Why a loop, not a long session — bounded context execution

The execute-loop is the operational shape of **bounded context execution** — sometimes called the **Ralph loop**. LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills: transcript, tool output, and failed attempts accumulate and compete with the original instructions. We call this decay **context rot**, and it is why long-running agent sessions are unreliable even on frontier models.

`ddx work` answers context rot structurally. Each bead runs in a fresh agent invocation with a clean context, scoped tightly to the bead's description, acceptance criteria, and the files it touches. When the attempt ends — merged, preserved, or abandoned — the context is discarded. Persistent state lands on disk as evidence, code, an updated bead, or a `<review-findings>` block threaded into the next attempt's prompt. The next iteration starts cold and reads what it needs from the substrate.

The result: drain throughput stays steady across hundreds of beads. The agent never has to remember what attempt 47 was thinking. The loop does the remembering, on disk, in plain files.

See [Bounded Context Execution](/docs/concepts/bounded-context-execution/) for the full treatment.
